coredata.dc:title: Nanoarchitectonics of Cu-derived bright carbon dots towards Cr (VI) and tetracycline hydrochloride detection
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Hao, Xuanqi
Zhang, Xiao
Liu, Chenchen
Yang, Ping
coredata.dc:description: Metal element doping often can improve the photoluminescence (PL) of carbon dots (CDs). In this paper, blue and green emitting CDs (BCDs and GCDs) were synthesized by using phenylenediamine as the carbon and nitrogen sources. Cu-doped CDs (Cu-CDs) were fabricated by using copper acetylacetone as a precursor. The PL peak wavelengths of sample Cu-doped blue CDs (Cu-BCDs) and green CDs (Cu-GCDs) were 450 and 543 nm, respectively. The full width at half maximum of PL spectra were 67.31 and 93.13 nm, the PL quantum yields (PLQYs) were 13.6 and 38.7 %, and the average fluorescence lifetimes were 4.34 and 20.97 ns, respectively. The PL intensity of the Cu-CDs samples was enhanced with the photostability, and thermal stability test results showed that the Cu-CDs samples also had excellent stability. The Cu-BCDs and Cu-GCDs were employed in the field of sensing applications due to their excellent selectivity towards Cr (VI) and tetracycline hydrochloride (TC) detection. The detection limits for Cr (VI) and TC using Cu-BCDs were 0.34 and 0.86 μM, while Cu-GCDs showed detection limits of 0.47 and 0.50 μM, respectively, demonstrating the broad application prospect for Cu-CDs in sensing applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Sensors
Cr (VI) ion
Tetracycline hydrochloride
Photoluminescence

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis procedure of Cu-BCDs and Cu-GCDs samples.
(a-c) TEM and (d, e) HRTEM images of Cu-GCDs sample. (f) Size distribution of Cu-GCDs sample.
(a) XPS full spectrum, (b) C 1s, (c) N 1s, (d) O 1s and (e) Cu 2p spectra of Cu-GCDs sample. (f) FTIR spectra of BCDs, Cu-BCDs, GCDs and Cu-GCDs samples.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of Cu-CDs samples. (b, c) PL spectra of sample Cu-BCDs and Cu-GCDs under various excitation wavelengths. (d) Fluorescent decay plots of the Cu-CDs samples. (e, f) Thermal stability and (g, h) photostability of the Cu-CDs samples. (i) PL intensity evolution of the Cu-CDs samples before and after UV light irradiation.

(a, c) PL spectra of Cu-BCDs and Cu-GCDs samples in different ionic, amino acid, and antibiotic solutions. (b, d) PL response of Cu-BCDs and Cu-GCDs to different solutions. The concentration of solutions was 1 mM.
(a, c) PL spectra of Cu-BCDs in Cr (VI) and TC solutions with different concentrations (0–300 μM). (b, d) Linear relationship of F/F0 with Cr (VI) and TC concentrations.
(a, c) PL spectra of Cu-GCDs in Cr (VI) and TC solutions with different concentrations (0–300 μM). (b, d) Linear relationship of F/F0 with Cr (VI) and TC concentrations.
(a, b) Absorption spectra of Cr (VI), TC and PL spectra of Cu-BCDs sample. (c, d) Fluorescence decay plots of Cu-BCDs sample in Cr (VI) and TC solutions.
(a, b) Absorption spectra of Cr (VI), TC and PL spectra of Cu-GCDs sample. (c, d) Fluorescence decay plots of Cu-GCDs sample in Cr (VI) and TC solutions.
PL properties of BCDs, Cu-BCDs, GCDs and Cu-GCDs samples.
Components B1 and B2, time constants τ1 and τ2, and average lifetime τaverage of Cu-BCDs and Cu-GCDs samples dissolved in ethanol, H2O, Cr (VI) and TC solutions.
Comparison of LOD for Cr (VI) using various CDs.

row.entry: Methyl glycine diacetic acid trisodium salt, ethylenediamine

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of LOD for TC using various CDs.

head.ce:title: Nanoarchitectonics of Cu-derived bright carbon dots towards Cr (VI) and tetracycline hydrochloride detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Material Science & Engineering, University of Jinan, 250022, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Material Science & Engineering, University of Jinan, 250022, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Cracow University of Technology, Warszawska 24 St. 31-155 Krakow, Poland

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Chemical Engineering and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Cracow University of Technology, Warszawska 24 St. 31-155 Krakow, Poland

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Metal element doping often can improve the photoluminescence (PL) of carbon dots (CDs). In this paper, blue and green emitting CDs (BCDs and GCDs) were synthesized by using phenylenediamine as the carbon and nitrogen sources. Cu-doped CDs (Cu-CDs) were fabricated by using copper acetylacetone as a precursor. The PL peak wavelengths of sample Cu-doped blue CDs (Cu-BCDs) and green CDs (Cu-GCDs) were 450 and 543 nm, respectively. The full width at half maximum of PL spectra were 67.31 and 93.13 nm, the PL quantum yields (PLQYs) were 13.6 and 38.7 %, and the average fluorescence lifetimes were 4.34 and 20.97 ns, respectively. The PL intensity of the Cu-CDs samples was enhanced with the photostability, and thermal stability test results showed that the Cu-CDs samples also had excellent stability. The Cu-BCDs and Cu-GCDs were employed in the field of sensing applications due to their excellent selectivity towards Cr (VI) and tetracycline hydrochloride (TC) detection. The detection limits for Cr (VI) and TC using Cu-BCDs were 0.34 and 0.86 μM, while Cu-GCDs showed detection limits of 0.47 and 0.50 μM, respectively, demonstrating the broad application prospect for Cu-CDs in sensing applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Compared to semiconductor quantum dots and organic dyes, metal free and nontoxic carbon dots (CDs) as spherical nanoparticles with a diameter of less than 10 nm CDs possess numerous advantages, including excellent biocompatibility, high stability, and facile functionalization [1–7]. Consequently, CDs have garnered extensive attention from researchers in recent years. CDs have been widely used in many fields, including light-emitting devices [8–12], photocatalysis and electrocatalysis [13,14], bioimaging [15] and detection [16–18]. The excellent fluorescence properties of CDs were favorable for the utilization of the material in fluorescent probe application. Since CDs often show excellent sensitivity and selectivity towards different ions and pollutants, the use of CDs in fluorescent probe application has been quite popular due to their facile synthesis, cost-effectiveness, and excellent selectivity nature.

The excessive presence of heavy metal ions (e.g. Fe3+, Cr (VI), Pb2+) in the natural environment (i.e., heavy metal ion pollution) poses a significant threat to soil and groundwater [19]. The excessive accumulation of heavy metals could cause significant harm to the environment. Since the contaminated soil and groundwater enter the biological body with the circulation of the ecosystem, endangering the health of the living organism, the detection of heavy metals can be imperative in fighting the environmental crisis. Currently, the predominant methods for detecting heavy metals include spectral detection technology, electrochemical detection technology, and optical detection technology [20]. Based on the data reported in literature, heavy metal ions detection using CDs can be a feasible approach. Chen et al. synthesized yellow-emitting CDs by employing o-phenylenediamine as the precursor for attaining sensitive detection of the Cr (VI) ions, achieving a remarkable detection limit of 0.64 μM [21]. K. Algethami et al. synthesized a N, S, O doped blue-emitting CDs using l-cysteine as the starting material [22], realizing selective detection of Cu ions, with a detection limit of 2 μM. Lin et al. synthesized blue emitting CDs (B-CDs) having the photoluminescence (PL) peak at 450 nm [23]. The fabricated B-CDs attained efficient detection of Fe3+ (which often used for fluorescence sensing in plants) with a detection limit of 0.83 μM.

The broad-spectrum antibiotic tetracycline hydrochloride (TC) often exhibits bactericidal effects at high concentrations. TC has been widely studied in applications such as, prevention of diseases in humans, aquaculture, and feed additive in animal husbandry. At the same time, the overuse of TC can cause numerous ecological issues and endanger the health of organisms [24]. Some of the common methods used for TC detection are high performance liquid chromatography, electrochemical analysis, surface enhanced Raman spectroscopy [25]. These detection methods are often disadvantaged by the complex operation and the requirement in large detection instruments. In comparison, visual detection of TC often can be easily attained by using the CDs having appropriate fluorescence properties. For example, Hu et al. synthesized a Zn-doped blue-fluorescent CDs (Zn-CDs) for selective detection of TC [25]. According to Hu et al., when TC solution was added to Zn-CDs solution, the intensity of PL peak at 440 nm was reduced, while a new PL peak was appeared at 515 nm. The Zn-CDs probe showed good selectivity for TC and a detection limit of 61.1 nM. Laddha et al. used papaya seeds and ethylenediamine as the precursors to synthesize yellow-emitting CDs (PS-CDs) via microwave treatment, attaining a low TC detection limit of 120 nM [26].

High PL efficiency can be significant in case of the utilization of CDs in sensor applications. In this paper, Cu-doped CDs were successfully fabricated with much improved PL intensity. Isomers of phenylenediamine (m-phenylenediamine and o-phenylenediamine) were used as the precursors for synthesizing the blue- and green-emitting CDs (BCDs and GCDs). Cu doping was the key to the improved optical properties (involving increased PL quantum yields (PLQYs), decreased full width at half maximum (FWHM), and enhanced stability) of the CDs. The preparation of bright Cu-doped CDs (Cu-BCDs and Cu-GCDs) was done by adjusting the doping amount of Cu. The PL emission peaks of Cu-BCDs and Cu-GCDs samples were 450 and 543 nm, respectively; and the PLQYs of the Cu-doped CDs (Cu-CDs) samples were 13.6 and 38.7 %; with a FWHM of 67.31 and 93.13 nm respectively. The estimated average fluorescence lifetimes of the Cu-CDs samples were 4.34 and 20.97 ns, demonstrating the excellent photostability and thermal stability of the Cu-CDs. After 180 min of continuous ultraviolet (UV, 365 nm) light irradiation treatment, the PL intensity of Cu-CDs was 133 and 107 % of the initial level; and after 10 min of heat treatment, the PL intensity of the samples was maintained at 137 and 138 % of the initial value. In addition, Cu-CDs can also be applied for antibiotic detection. Experimental results demonstrated that Cu-CDs exhibited excellent selectivity towards Cr (VI) and TC. The detection limits of Cu-BCDs for Cr (VI) and TC were 0.34 and 0.86 μM, respectively; while the detection limits of Cu-GCDs for Cr (VI) and TC were 0.47 and 0.50 μM, respectively.

M-phenylenediamine (M-PD, 99.5 %), o-phenylenediamine (O-PD, 98.5 %), and l-serine (99 %) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Ethanol (99.7 %) was supplied from Tianjin Fuyu Chemical Co., Ltd. Copper acetylacetonate (99.9 %), l-arginine (98 %), l-proline (99 %), l-tryptophan (99 %) and oxytetracycline hydrochloride (OTC, 95 %) were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. Glycine (99 %) was purchased from Tianjin Kemiou Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. L-phenylalanine (98.5 %) was supplied by Tianjin Guangfu Fine Chemical Research Institute. Tetracycline hydrochloride (TC, 98 %) was supplied by Hefei Qiansheng Biotechnology Co., Ltd. Carbamazepine (CBZ, 98 %) and Ciprofloxacin (CIP, 98 %) was purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. The deionized water was obtained using Millipore system (France). All reagents were directly used without any further purification.

10 mg of M/O-PD was dispersed into 20 mL of ethanol solution under 10 min of sonication. The mixed solution was transferred to a 50 mL polytetrafluoroethylene lined autoclave and heat-treated at 180 °C for 10 or 12 h. The treated solution was filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membrane to eliminate large impurity particles before collecting the transparent CDs solution. The resulting samples were named as BCDs and GCDs. Cu-CDs were synthesized using the same method as that of CDs, but with different synthetic materials. 6.4 mg of copper acetylacetonate was added to the precursor, following the same subsequent procedure as that for preparing the CDs. The samples obtained were denoted as Cu-BCD and Cu-GCD.

To study the selectivity of Cu-CDs towards anions, cations, amino acid, and antibiotic solutions, 1 mL of the test solution with a concentration of 1 mM was mixed with 30 μL of Cu-CDs solution. The PL spectra demonstrated that the fabricated Cu-CDs exhibited excellent selectivity towards Cr (VI) and TC. In order to evaluate the response of Cu-BCDs and Cu-GCDs samples to Cr (VI) and TC, 30 μL of Cu-BCDs and Cu-GCDs solution was mixed with 1 mL of Cr (VI) and TC solution having different concentrations ranging from 0 to 300 μM (0, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 40, 50, 100, 150, 200, 250, 300 μM). Moreover, in order to analyze the linear relation between the solution concentration and the PL intensity (F/F0) of the sample, the detection limits of Cu-BCDs and Cu-GCDs for Cr (VI) and TC were calculated using the following formula: LOD = 3δ/k, where δ represents the deviation of the response value, and k represents the linear slope of the fitted curve.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of CDs were examined using a FEI Tecnai F20 microscope. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra of CDs component elements were measured by Thermo Kaipha spectrometer. The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of CDs were analyzed using Nicolet 380 spectrometer. The UV–visible (vis) absorption and PL spectra of CDs were determined using Hitachi U-4100 and Hitachi F-4600 fluorescence spectrometers. The fluorescence decay plots of CDs were tested using Jobin Yvon IBH fluorescence lifetime analyzer. The photostability of CDs was assessed using WFH-204B UV analyzer. The PLQYs of samples were estimated by comparing with rhodamine 6G in an ethanol solution with a PLQYs of 95 %, and the detailed information are shown in supporting information.

Blue and green emitting CDs (BCDs and GCDs) were prepared via solvothermal synthesis using M-PD and O-PD as the precursors. The Cu-doped blue- and green-emitting CDs (Cu-BCDs and Cu-GCDs) with enhanced PLQYs were successfully synthesized by employing copper acetylacetonate as the Cu source. The PLQYs and stability of CDs were enhanced by Cu doping. The detailed synthesis procedure of the Cu-doped CDs (Cu-CDs) samples is illustrated in Fig. 1 , with the precursors and the controlled parameters been the keys for enhancing the PL performance of CDs.

The morphology of Cu-GCDs was examined using TEM. Fig. 2 a-2e show the TEM and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images of Cu-GCDs sample at different magnifications. The Cu-GCDs prepared via solvothermal synthesis exhibited distinct lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) plane spacing of graphitic carbon (Fig. 2e) [27]. Cu-GCDs showed excellent crystallinity, while Cu-GCDs showed monodisperse quasi-spherical morphology with an average size of 3.17 ± 2.5 nm (Fig. 2f).

The elemental composition and surface functional group composition of Cu-GCDs were analyzed using XPS and FTIR. Based on previous studies, the CDs synthesized by M- and O-PD were mainly composed of C, N, and O elements [12]. The XPS survey spectrum with five peaks corresponding to the C 1s, N 1s, O 1s, Cu LMM, and Cu 2p peaks are shown in Fig. 3 a [28–30]. Fig. 3a showed that Cu-GCDs was mainly composed of C, N, O and Cu elements, with a composition ratio of 75.18, 13.97, 9.24 and 1.83 %, respectively. The C 1s spectrum in Fig. 3b showed emission peaks at 284.4, 285.9, and 288.8 eV corresponding to the C–C/C=C, C-N/C-O, and C=N/C=O bonds, respectively. Fig. 3c showed the N 1s spectrum, with the peaks of pyridine N, pyrrole N, and graphite N located at 399.2, 400.6, and 401.9 eV, respectively. In case of O 1s spectrum obtained (Fig. 3d), the peaks observed at 531.3 and 532.9 eV were due to the presence of C-O and C=O bonds. Fig. 3e shows the spectrum of Cu 2p. The four peaks were fitted as Cu+ 2p3/2 (932.8 eV), Cu2+ 2p3/2 (934.9 eV), Cu+ 2p1/2 (952.6 eV) and Cu2+ 2p1/2 (955.4 eV) [24,31]. XPS results confirmed the successful incorporation of Cu in the material. The FTIR spectra showed the composition of functional groups on the CDs sample surfaces (Fig. 3f). The stretching vibration band of 3050–3500 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration associated with the N–H/O–H bonds. The stretching vibration band located at 2800–3000 cm−1 was attributed to the C–H bond, and the stretching vibration band located near 1650 cm−1 was related to the C=O and C=C bonds. Meanwhile, the stretching vibration peak at 1250 and 1030 cm−1 were ascribed to the C-N and Cu–N bond stretching vibration, respectively. It was demonstrated that there existed many functional groups on the surface of CDs. The presence of Cu–N bond confirmed that Cu ions were successfully coordinated onto the surfaces or in-between the carbon layers of the materials. Moreover, the FITR results also showed that Cu elements were successfully incorporated onto the surfaces of Cu-CDs via covalent bonding [32–35].

The optical properties of Cu-CDs were subsequently characterized. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the absorption spectra of Cu-BCDs exhibited absorption bands at 245 and 355 nm, and Cu-GCDs exhibited absorption bands at 241, 265 and 380 nm due to the π-π* electronic transition of the C=C bond and the n-π* electronic transition of the C=O bond [36].

The PL properties of Cu-CDs samples adhere to the stokes rule, the emission wavelength exceeds the excitation wavelength, and PL spectra exhibit excitation-independent characteristics. Cu-BCDs and Cu-GCDs samples exhibited excitation at wavelengths of 320–400 and 360–440 nm (Fig. 4b and 4c). The PL peaks of Cu-CDs samples were observed at 450 and 543 nm with no shift in the PL center. The PL spectra showed a symmetrical distribution, indicating that Cu-BCDs and Cu-GCDs samples under examination emitted fluorescence of a single color. Table 1 illustrates that the maximized excitation wavelengths for Cu-BCDs and Cu-GCDs were 396 and 381 nm. Additionally, the PL peaks were observed at 450 and 543 nm. The FWHM of Cu-BCDs was 67.31 nm, which was reduced by about 8.1 nm compared with BCDs, and the FWHM of Cu-GCDs was 93.13 nm, which was reduced by about 1.4 nm. The PLQYs of CDs and Cu-CDs were calculated based on the results obtained using ethanol solution rhodamine 6G [5]. The standard curve of PLQYs for BCDs is shown in Fig. S4 (see Supporting Information). Similar method was also applied on other CDs samples. The PLQYs of Cu-BCDs were increased from 9.4 to 13.6 %, while Cu-GCDs had also experienced an increase from 32.5 to 38.7 %. The PLQYs of Cu-BCDs and Cu-GCDs were affected by many factors. The precursor and reaction time of Cu-BCDs and Cu-GCDs were different, resulting in different quantum yields obtained for the CDs. The high quantum yield of Cu-GCDs compared to that of Cu-BCDs demonstrated the decrease in non-recombination of Cu-GCDs.

The fluorescent decay plots of Cu-BCDs and Cu-GCDs samples were also tested (Fig. 4d). The fluorescence lifetime is the time that a molecule remained in an excited state after being excited. The modes of molecular returned from the excited state to the ground state involved both radiative recombination and non-radiative recombination, where radiative recombination corresponded to the long components τ2 while non-radiative recombination corresponded to the short components τ1. The fluorescence lifetimes of Cu-CDs were determined using the τ = (B1 * τ 1 2 + B2* τ 2 2) / (B1* τ 1 + B2* τ 2) formula through double exponential fitting, yielding values of 4.34 and 20.97 ns respectively (Table 2 ) [37].

Stability is an important factor in evaluating luminescent materials. Therefore, the thermal and optical stability of Cu-BCDs and Cu-GCDs samples was tested. The Cu-CDs samples dispersed in ethanol was heated at 78 °C for 10 min, followed by cooling to room temperature to test the PL spectra of the samples before and after heating (Fig. 4e and 4f). The PL intensity of Cu-BCDs and Cu-GCDs samples after heat treatment were 1.37 and 1.38 times, which were higher than the initial intensity. After heat treatment, the concentration of Cu-CDs increased, leading to enhanced PL intensity of the sample. The photostability of Cu-CDs samples were measured and the PL intensity of the Cu-CDs samples were recorded before and after 180 min of continuous UV light treatment (Fig. 4g and 4 h). After UV light irradiation, the PL intensity of Cu-CDs samples remained 1.33 and 1.07 times of the initial value (Fig. 4i). The results demonstrated that Cu-CDs samples exhibited exceptional thermal and optical stability, thereby providing a solid theoretical foundation for further applications. The optical response of the CDs at different pH conditions was also tested. Fig. S2a showed the PL spectra of sample BCDs and Cu-BCDs at different pH conditions. A decrease in PL intensity of sample BCDs was observed in both acidic and alkaline media. In case of sample Cu-BCDs, the PL intensity remained almost the same at a pH range of 2 to 7. However, a rapid decrease in PL intensity was observed when pH > 7, with the PL of sample Cu-BCDs almost quenched. Fig. S2b shows the PL spectra of sample GCDs and Cu-GCDs under different pH conditions. The PL intensity of sample GCDs and Cu-GCDs decreased in both acidic and alkaline media. And this was attributed to the change of surface functional groups observed on CDs at different pH conditions.

To investigate the response of CDs and Cu-CDs samples to different ionic, amino acid, antibiotic solutions. CDs solutions was added to 1 mL of different ionic, amino acid and antibiotic solutions. Fig. 5 a and 5b show the PL response of Cu-BCDs to different test samples. Fig. 5c and 5d show the PL responses of Cu-GCDs to different test samples. Fig. S3 shows the PL response of sample BCDs and GCDs. The presence of Cr (VI) and TC resulted in the decrease in PL intensity of BCDs and GCDs. Sample Cu-BCDs and Cu-GCDs revealed the better selectivity for Cr (VI) and TC, in comparison with that of BCDs and GCDs. Fig. S4 shows the digital pictures of sample Cu-BCDs and Cu-GCDs dispersed in various ionic, amino acid and antibiotic solution. The presence of Cr (VI) and TC caused the fluorescence quenching of Cu-BCDs and Cu-GCDs, indicating the decent sensitivity and selectivity of Cu-CDs towards Cr (VI) and TC. The detection limits of Cu-BCDs and Cu-GCDs were separately evaluated for Cr (VI) and TC. Cu-CDs samples were dispersed in Cr (VI) and TC solutions with varying concentrations to test PL response of Cu-CDs to different concentrations.

Fig. 6 a and 6b show the PL response spectra of Cu-BCDs to Cr (VI) ions. The PL intensity exhibited a gradual decrease as the concentration of Cr (VI) increased. Cu-BCDs exhibit fluorescence quenching due to the presence of Cr (VI), indicating excellent selectivity for Cr (VI). With the concentration of Cr (VI) ranged from 0 to 50 μL, the PL intensity F/F0 of the sample exhibited a linear correlation with the Cr (VI) concentration (Fig. 6b). The corresponding linear equation was y = -0.0088x + 1.0181, with a correlation coefficient of R2 = 0.9901 and a detection limit of 0.34 μM.

The same method was applied to assess the selectivity of Cu-BCDs towards TC. Fig. 6c and 6d showed the relation of PL spectra of Cu-BCDs with TC concentration. The concentration range of TC was 0–50 μL, and a strong linear relation existed between PL intensity F/F0 and TC concentration (Fig. 6d). The linear equation was y = -0.0035x + 0.9927, with R2 = 0.9973, indicating excellent linearity. In addition, the detection limit of Cu-BCDs for TC was 0.86 μM. Cu-GCDs also exhibited favorable selectivity towards Cr (VI) and TC.

Fig. 7 a and 7b showed the PL response of Cu-GCDs to Cr (VI). Fig. 7b showed that a good linear correlation was observed between the PL intensity F/F0 and Cr (VI) concentration having a Cr (VI) concentration range of 0 to 50 μL. The fitted linear equation was y = -0.0064x + 0.9587, with a R2 value of 0.9910, and the detection limit was 0.47 μM. The PL response of Cu-GCDs for TC is shown in Fig. 7c and 7d. Within the range of 0–50 μL, the PL intensity F/F0 exhibited a strong linear correlation with TC concentration (Fig. 6d), described by the equation y = -0.0060x + 1.0063 with a R2 value of 0.9908 and a corresponding detection limit of 0.50 μM.

The work presented here was also compared to those reported in literature that utilized different synthesis methods and starting material for CDs fabrication. Table 3 illustrates the comparison between the detection limit of Cu-CDs for Cr (VI) and the literature reported works. Cu-BCDs had a lower detection limit for Cr (VI), indicating that Cu-BCDs could achieve selective detection of Cr (VI). Table 4 illustrates the comparison of the detection limits of Cu-GCDs for TC with the results reported in literature, suggesting that Cu-GCDs revealed high selectivity for TC. CDs prepared using different precursors and synthetic methodologies showed different surface functional group compositions. The different selectivity of CDs towards Cr (VI) was due to the different surface functional groups on CDs.

The fluorescence quenching mechanism of Cu-CDs on Cr (VI) and TC was investigated. Fig. S5 showed the detection mechanism of Cr (VI) and TC using Cu-CDs. The quenching mechanism of CDs could be divided into static quenching, dynamic quenching and inner filter effect (IFE) [55–58]. IFE referred to the phenomenon that the absorber absorbs PL spectra of the fluorescent material, leading to decrease in PL intensity of the fluorescent material.

The occurrence of IFE was observed when the absorption spectra of quenchers Cr (VI) and TC overlapped with the PL spectra of Cu-CDs. The absorption spectra of Cr (VI) and TC shown in Fig. 8 a and 8b exhibited the overlapping regions with the PL spectra of Cu-BCDs. And the fluorescence quenching of Cu-BCDs was due to the IFE. Moreover, the effects of Cr (VI) and TC on the fluorescence lifetime of Cu-BCDs were also analyzed. Fig. 8c and 8d showed the fluorescence lifetime of sample Cu-BCDs in different solutions, including H2O, Cr (VI) and TC. As illustrated in Table 2, the average fluorescence lifetimes of sample Cu-BCDs in H2O, Cr (VI) and TC solutions were 8.55, 8.16 and 8.41 ns respectively. The fluorescence lifetime of Cu-BCDs was minimally affected by Cr (VI) and TC, indicating the quenching of Cu-BCDs was caused by static quenching. Similarly, the fluorescence quenching mechanism of Cu-GCDs in Cr (VI) and TC was analyzed.

The absorption spectra of Cr (VI) and TC in Fig. 9 a and 9b showed overlapping regions with PL spectra of Cu-GCDs, indicating the fluorescence quenching of Cu-GCDs was attributed to the IFE. In addition, Fig. 9c and 9d showed the fluorescence decay plots of Cu-GCDs in H2O, Cr (VI) and TC. The average fluorescence lifetimes of Cu-GCDs in H2O, Cr (VI) and TC were 6.91, 6.70 and 7.73 ns, respectively (Table 2). The effects of Cr (VI) and TC on the fluorescence lifetime of Cu-GCDs were negligible, indicating the fluorescence quenching of Cu-GCDs to be attributed to static quenching.

By using M-PD and O-PD as the precursors, blue- and green-emitting CDs were synthesized via solvothermal treatment. The addition of Cu improved the PL property of the CDs. The PL peak wavelengths of the Cu-BCDs and Cu-GCDs samples were observed at 450 and 540 nm, with the PLQYs of the samples estimated to be 13.6 and 38.7 %, respectively. Moreover, sample Cu-BCDs and Cu-GCDs showed great potential in the application of Cr (VI) and TC detection. The detection limits of Cu-BCDs for Cr (VI) and TC were estimated to be 0.34 and 0.86 μM, while the detection limits obtained for Cu-GCDs were 0.47 and 0.50 μM, respectively. It was obvious that Cu-BCDs showed a lower detection limit for Cr (VI), while the Cu-GCDs showed better selectivity for TC. The quenching mechanism of Cu-CDs were ascribed to the IFE and static quenching. These findings suggested that Cu-CDs possess great capability in ions and antibiotics detection, demonstrating their great potential in sensing applications.

Xuanqi Hao: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Xiao Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Data curation, Conceptualization. Chenchen Liu: Investigation, Formal analysis. Ping Yang: Writing – original draft, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported in part by the projects from the National Natural Science Foundation of China, PR China, (grant no. 51972145), the Independent Innovation Team from Ji Nan Science & Technology Bureau, Jinan, China (grant no, 2021GXRC109).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2024.162217.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
